mnemonics.title: Structure = Apartment, Union = Time-share
mnemonics.content: A structure is like an apartment building where each member gets its own room (separate memory). A union is like a time-share vacation home where all members take turns using the same single room (shared memory). Only one guest (member) can occupy it at a time.

mnemonics.title: S.U.I.T. - Structures Unite Individual Types
mnemonics.content: S.U.I.T. stands for Structures Unite Individual Types. A structure brings together different data types (int, float, char, etc.) into one cohesive unit, just like a suit brings together jacket, shirt, and pants into one outfit.

mnemonics.title: Dot for Direct, Arrow for Address
mnemonics.content: Use the Dot (.) operator for Direct access to structure members. Use the Arrow (->) operator when you have an Address (pointer). Both start with the same letter: Dot-Direct, Arrow-Address.

mnemonics.title: typedef: Type Definition Made Easy
mnemonics.content: typedef is like a nickname for a complex type. It simplifies code by creating an alias for a type, making declarations shorter and easier to read.

mnemonics.title: Padding: Pick a Larger Size
mnemonics.content: When calculating structure size, remember that padding is added to align members to memory boundaries. Arrange members from largest to smallest to minimize padding.

mnemonics.title: Self-Referential: Structure Points to Itself
mnemonics.content: A self-referential structure contains a pointer to its own type, forming the basis for linked lists, trees, and graphs. This is like a mirror reflecting itself.
